title: Collective Excitations and Stability of Nonequilibrium Polariton Supersolids
authors: A. Grudinina, J. Cao, A. Kavokin, N. Voronova, A. Nalitov
abstract: Formation of nonequilibrium counterparts of supersolids, simultaneously characterized with spontaneous superfluid and crystalline order, was recently reported in incoherently pumped polariton condensates. We investigate collective excitation spectra of this phase and explicitly demonstrate the emergence of gapless Nambu-Goldstone modes due to spontaneously broken continuous phase and translation symmetries. For the recent implementation of the polariton nonequilibrium supersolidity in semiconductor metasurfaces [D. Trypogeorgos et al., Nature 639, 337 (2025)], we demonstrate the key role of attractive polariton interactions, mediated by the excitonic reservoir, for stability of the supersolid phase. Performing a thorough numerical investigation, we identify the conditions for existence of the diagonal and off-diagonal long-range order in negative-mass nonequilibrium supersolids.
summary: This paper explores the stability and excitations of a recently reported nonequilibrium polariton supersolid. The authors show that while the system's negative effective mass would typically lead to instability, the interaction with an incoherent exciton reservoir renormalizes the polariton interactions to a stabilizing regime. They identify the appearance of two distinct gapless Nambu-Goldstone modes as the definitive signature of the supersolid state. The work provides a theoretical foundation for understanding how dissipation and reservoir effects can drive and stabilize complex broken-symmetry phases.
main_problem: Investigating the collective excitation spectra and dynamical stability of nonequilibrium polariton supersolids, specifically addressing how the phase remains stable despite a negative effective mass.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the supersolid phase is stabilized by attractive polariton interactions mediated by an incoherent exciton reservoir, which renormalizes the interaction strength to prevent collapse. It also identifies the emergence of two gapless Nambu-Goldstone modes as the smoking gun for the supersolid phase.
method: Mean-field analysis using coupled Gross-Pitaevskii equations (GPEs) combined with a fluctuation analysis (Bogoliubov approach) and Lyapunov exponent calculation.
model_or_system: A nonequilibrium system of exciton-polariton condensates in a Bound States in the Continuum (BiC) nanostructure, modeled via coupled GPEs for three polariton modes (0, +1, and -1) interacting with an incoherent exciton reservoir.
key_observables: Collective excitation spectra (real and imaginary parts), Nambu-Goldstone modes, Lyapunov exponents, and order parameter densities.
important_parameters: Pump power (W), effective interaction strength (g_eff), thresholds (W_th1, W_th2), and effective mass.
main_assumptions: Adiabatic reservoir limit, quasi-1D approximation along the waveguide axis, and truncation of the fluctuation matrix to the first several bands.
figures_summary: Schematics of the nanostructured waveguide and energy dispersion; plots of density and phase distributions; and the excitation spectrum showing the transition from one to two gapless modes and the stability phase diagram.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical platform and the OPO-driven supersolid mechanism, presents the theoretical model and GPE framework, analyzes the excitation spectra and symmetry breaking, and concludes with a stability analysis and phase diagram.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental insight into how reservoir-mediated interactions can stabilize exotic non-equilibrium phases (supersolids) in systems with inherently unstable (negative mass) dispersions, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and non-equilibrium many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the system. (a) The nanostructured waveguide with the grating period a. Layers of different colors show embedded QWs; the wavy lines illus- trate fundamental photonic modes of the structure propagat- ing in the x direction, TE±0 (purple) and TE±1 (blue). (b) The real parts of the dispersion laws ε(p) of the 0 branch and E±1(p) of the ±1 branches are shown with the solid purple and blue lines, respectively. The dashed lines correspond to the photonic modes sketched in (a). The thick orange line in- dicates the exciton resonance. (c) Sketch of the condensation at k = 0 of the BiC branch (the dashed purple line) reached after the first threshold. (d) Sketch of...

FIG. 2. (a) Density of the order parameter components n0 (purple) and n1 (blue) vs. the pump power W. The dot- ted gray line indicates the value of W at which the effec- tive interaction changes sign, geff(W) < 0, and the inter- action of negative-mass polaritons effectively becomes repul- sive; the yellow and lilac dashed lines mark the first and sec- ond thresholds. The inset shows phase boundaries for the condensed phase (NESF, yellow) and the supersolid phase (NESS, purple). The black dashed line indicates the value of the 0-mode Hopfield coefficient at which the calculations presented in the paper are performed. The PL intensity in reciprocal space for the NESF (b) and the NESS (c)...

FIG. 3. The excitation spectra Ek as a function of kx. Real (a) and imaginary (b) parts of Ek in the first band for the NESS (the purple, magenta, and lavender lines) and NESF (the yellow lines) phases. The solid lines in panels (a, b) correspond to the NG modes (see text), the dashed lines indi- cate the gapped modes. Parameters above the 2nd threshold: W = 1.05 Wth2, µ = 1 meV, n0 = 520 µm2, n1/n0 = 2×10−4; below the 2nd threshold: W = 2.5 Wth1 = 0.64 Wth2, n0 = 420 µm−2, µ = 0.9 meV. The insets in panels (a) and (b) show the smaller momentum range enlarged.
